message.content:
You are critically reviewing the design capture for recipe-bottle (RBK) heat ₣BZ
("rbk-14-citizen-model"). Two artifacts: a PADDOCK (shape only) and a MECHANICS MEMO (detail).

The PADDOCK (current committed text) is reproduced verbatim here:
----- PADDOCK BEGIN -----
undefined
----- PADDOCK END -----

The MECHANICS MEMO is the file Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md — READ IT.

Context you must hold:
- "Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-tier operator identity (a depot-issued
  service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's identity is a "federate."
  The payor is the founder, outside both tiers.
- The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle; capabilities are named
  "capability-sets" (IAM grant bundles in code); intent is stored in a "declared roster";
  an audit diffs declared-vs-IAM.
- The paddock holds SHAPE; the memo holds MECHANISM. Scoping/heat-chopping is INTENTIONALLY
  deferred — do NOT file "decide the scope" as a finding; that is a known open.
- A prior review pass already corrected ~11 issues, and the text above already reflects them.
  Do NOT re-report things already fixed in the current text. Focus on improvements NOT yet
  present. If you believe an already-present claim is wrong, say so explicitly.

Source documents you may cross-check (READ the ones relevant to your lens):
- Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (committed two-tier credential plan; the model claims to supersede it)
- Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md (credential lifecycle split — the enabler)
- Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md (standalone governor-mantle tombstone-leak fix)
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc ("Operator federation" + envoy/embassy roadmap entries)
- cult-verb specs under Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/: Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDR-director_roster.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc

Rules: Be a genuine, skeptical critic — only report REAL, substantiated issues, each with a
concrete fix. Cite evidence precisely (a quote, a spec name, a file:area, or a URL). It is fine
to find few issues. Do NOT pad. Prefer high-value findings. Your final output is structured data,
not a human message.

ADVERSARIALLY VERIFY this candidate finding. Independently check its evidence — READ the cited file/spec, or WEB-SEARCH the cited fact; do NOT trust the finding's own evidence text. Default to verdict "false" if you cannot substantiate it. If it is real but the stated fix is wrong or imprecise, mark "partial" and give a corrected refined_fix.

FINDING (JSON):
{"title":"Divest decomposition omits the roster write — leaving a 'declared but revoked' deficit that auto-converge will resurrect","location":"memo §Verb dissolution (line 49: 'divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen') vs §The audit auto-converge","type":"security","severity":"high","issue":"Verb dissolution decomposes divest into 'revoke(all held) + remove-citizen' (line 49) but does not state that remove-citizen *also deletes the roster entry*, and the ordering is unspecified. If the roster row survives a divest (or is deleted after a revoke but a residual capability-set row remains), the audit sees declared-but-absent = deficit = auto-converge, and re-grants capabilities to a citizen you just tore down. This is the same monotonic-accumulation class as the H1 binding-leak (memo-20260604) but worse: it actively *re-provisions* a revoked identity. The asymmetric-healing design assumes the roster is always the authoritative present intent, so any path that revokes IAM without synchronously zeroing the roster row turns auto-heal into auto-resurrection. The current divest spec (RBSDD) is fail-loud and does not touch any roster (rosters today are derived/emitted read-only per RBSDR), so the new model introduces a write-ordering obligation that the verb decomposition does not pin down.","evidence":"memo line 49 'divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen'; memo lines 105-112 auto-converge any declared-but-absent deficit; RBSDD-director_divest.adoc revokes bindings and deletes the SA but never writes a roster (RBSDR is read-only emission). No step sequences roster-delete before/with revoke.","suggested_fix":"Specify divest as: delete the roster row(s) FIRST (intent withdrawn), THEN revoke IAM, THEN remove the citizen — so no window exists where declared>actual makes auto-converge re-grant a torn-down identity. Add an explicit invariant: 'no IAM revoke without a prior or atomic roster withdrawal,' the dual of 'grant writes intent first.' Call out the auto-resurrection failure mode as the reason ordering is load-bearing.","confidence":"high"}

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by reading the mechanics memo and the cited specs.

content.content:
1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
8	independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).
9	
10	## Scope and vocabulary
11	
12	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
13	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
14	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
15	
16	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
17	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
18	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
19	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
20	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
21	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
22	  federate (memo-20260527).
23	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
24	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
25	- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
26	  Tier-blind.
27	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
28	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
29	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
30	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
31	  ontological category is deferred).
32	
33	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
34	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
35	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
36	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
37	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
38	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
39	
40	## Verb dissolution
41	
42	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
43	(actor, capability-set, identity):
44	
45	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
48	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
49	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
50	| roster | read the declared roster | |
51	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
52	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
53	
54	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
55	half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
56	
57	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
58	
59	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.
134	- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.
135	
136	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
137	
138	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
139	can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:
140	
141	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
142	  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
143	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
144	  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.
145	
146	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
147	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
148	
149	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
150	
151	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
152	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
153	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
154	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
155	  honor-system.
156	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
157	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
158	  may grant/revoke:
159	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
160	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
161	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
162	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
163	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
164	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
165	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
166	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
167	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
168	  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
169	- **Composed resolution.**
170	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
171	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
172	    residual.
173	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
174	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
175	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
176	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
177	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
178	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
179	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
180	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
181	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
182	  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
183	  wanted.
184	
185	## Blast radius — full reasoning
186	
187	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
188	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
189	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
190	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
191	key; they are one decision.
192	
193	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
194	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
195	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
196	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
197	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
198	  one-per-role).
199	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
200	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
201	
202	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
203	
204	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
205	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
206	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
207	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
208	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
209	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
210	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
211	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
212	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
213	
214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
215	
216	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
217	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
218	  label.
219	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
220	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
221	
222	Sources:
223	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
224	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
225	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
226	
227	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
228	
229	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
230	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
231	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
232	
233	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
234	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
235	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
236	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
237	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
238	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
239	
240	Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.
241

1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_director_divest}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation tears down a
5	{rbtr_director}
6	{giam_service_account}
7	from the
8	{rbtgi_depot_project}.
9	The
10	{rbtr_governor}
11	synthesizes the SA email from the supplied identity folio, *revokes the SA's own IAM bindings across every scope it was granted*, then issues a REST DELETE against the IAM API, and opportunistically removes the production RBRA file if its `client_email` matches the synthesized email.
12	Revoking the live member before the delete is what keeps the binding leak closed: a member removed while still live leaves no `deleted:...?uid=` ghost, whereas deleting the SA first would have GCP retag its bindings as unbounded tombstones.
13	A required identity folio (param1) is supplied by the operator; the SA email is composed as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}`.
14	
15	//axhos_step
16	. *Authenticate as
17	{rbtr_governor}*:
18	.. Execute
19	{rbtoe_governor_authenticate}
20	.. {rbbc_store}
21	`«GOVERNOR_TOKEN»` for subsequent API calls
22	
23	//axhos_step
24	. *Synthesize Service Account Email*:
25	.. {rbbc_require}
26	identity folio (param1) is non-empty
27	.. {rbbc_store}
28	`«SA_EMAIL»` as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}` (composed from the role prefix and the operator-supplied identity)
29	
30	//axhos_step
31	. *Revoke
32	{rbtr_director}
33	IAM Bindings*:
34	.. NOTE: Run before the DELETE so each live member is removed while the SA still exists — no `deleted:...?uid=` tombstone is minted on any resource. Mirrors exactly the bindings granted by {rbtgo_director_invest}; the {rbtr_mason} `roles/artifactregistry.writer` binding is Mason's, not the director's, and is left intact. See the member-removal contract in <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>.
35	.. Remove `serviceAccount:«SA_EMAIL»` from the
36	{rbtgi_depot_project}
37	IAM policy, roles:
38	... `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor`
39	... `roles/viewer`
40	... `roles/cloudbuild.workerPoolUser`
41	.. Remove `serviceAccount:«SA_EMAIL»` with `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` from the
42	{rbtr_mason}
43	service account IAM policy
44	.. Remove `serviceAccount:«SA_EMAIL»` with `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` from the
45	{rbtgi_repository}
46	IAM policy
47	.. Policy version: 3; a binding left with no members is pruned
48	.. {rbbc_fatal}
49	on revoke failure — teardown is fail-loud: a non-OK policy status or an HTTP 409 etag conflict (single-writer invariant) aborts the divest before the SA is deleted; the operator reruns. The {rbtgi_repository} and {rbtr_mason} revokes presume those standing resources exist (they outlive director SAs); a missing resource there surfaces only during a full depot teardown.
50	.. NOTE: Idempotent — removing an absent member is a no-op, so a re-divest revokes cleanly without error.
51	
52	//axhos_step
53	. *Divest Director Service Account*:
54	.. {rbbc_call}
55	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/delete[iam.projects.serviceAccounts.delete] with:
56	... method: `DELETE`
57	... endpoint: `https://iam.googleapis.com/v1/projects/{rbtgi_depot_project}/serviceAccounts/«SA_EMAIL»`
58	... header: `Authorization: Bearer «GOVERNOR_TOKEN»`
59	.. {rbbc_require}
60	HTTP 200 response OR HTTP 404 (already divested)
61	.. {rbbc_warn}
62	if HTTP 404: "Service account not found (already divested)"
63	
64	//axhos_step
65	. *Confirm Deletion Propagated*:
66	.. {rbbc_poll}
67	the SA GET endpoint until it returns HTTP 404 for `RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK` consecutive observations, confirming the
68	{giam_service_account}
69	is durably gone. A delete-accepted response precedes propagation, and the post-delete read path flaps 404→200 across replicas (see <<rbscip_trade_study>>), so a single 404 is not durable proof — an immediately following same-name
70	{rbtgo_director_invest}
71	would otherwise race a still-visible account. Any intervening non-404 resets the streak.
72	.. NOTE: skipped when the DELETE returned HTTP 404 — the account was already absent, so there is nothing to wait for
73	.. {rbbc_require}
74	`RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK` consecutive HTTP 404s within the eventual-consistency deadline
75	
76	//axhos_step
77	. *Opportunistic RBRA Cleanup*:
78	.. If the role's RBRA file exists at
79	{rbrr_director_rbra_file},
80	source it and read `RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL`
81	.. If `RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL` equals `«SA_EMAIL»`, remove the file
82	.. NOTE: The removal is conditional — if the installed RBRA's `client_email` does not match the divested `«SA_EMAIL»` (e.g., a different identity is currently installed), the file is left in place. No fatal error is raised when the file is absent or non-matching.
83	
84	//axhos_step
85	. *Display Result*:
86	.. {rbbc_show}
87	single-line success naming `«SA_EMAIL»` and confirming the
88	{rbtr_director}
89	{giam_service_account}
90	was divested, or a notice that it was already absent
91	
92	//axhoc_completion
93	Successful execution revokes the
94	{rbtr_director}
95	{giam_service_account}'s IAM bindings across every scope it held (project,
96	{rbtr_mason},
97	{rbtgi_repository}) and then removes it from
98	{rbtgi_depot_project}, leaving no `deleted:` ghost on any policy.
99	If the installed RBRA at
100	{rbrr_director_rbra_file}
101	matches the divested identity, it is also removed; otherwise it is left intact.
102	A 404 response from the cloud DELETE is accepted as success — the
103	{giam_service_account}
104	is absent either way.
105	Proceed to
106	{rbtgo_director_invest}
107	to re-issue a fresh
108	{rbtr_director}
109	{giam_service_account}
110	when needed.
111

1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_director_roster}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation rosters
5	{rbtr_director}
6	{giam_service_account_s}
7	in the
8	{rbtgi_depot_project}
9	via per-SA fact-file emission.
10	Read-only observation: lists all SAs, filters to director-prefixed emails, and emits one fact per discovered SA via
11	{buf_write_fact_multi}
12	against the `RBCC_fact_ext_roster_director` extension constant.
13	Fact value is the full SA email; `file_root` is the bare identity extracted from the email.
14	
15	//axhos_step
16	. *Authenticate as
17	{rbtr_governor}*:
18	.. Execute
19	{rbtoe_governor_authenticate}
20	.. {rbbc_store}
21	`«GOVERNOR_TOKEN»` from the resulting access token
22	
23	//axhos_step
24	. *List service accounts*:
25	.. {rbbc_call}
26	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/list[iam.projects.serviceAccounts.list] with:
27	... method: `GET`
28	... endpoint: `https://iam.googleapis.com/v1/projects/{rbtgi_depot_project}/serviceAccounts`
29	... header: `Authorization: Bearer «GOVERNOR_TOKEN»`
30	.. {rbbc_require}
31	HTTP 200 response
32	.. {rbbc_store}
33	`«ACCOUNTS»` from response `.accounts[]` array
34	
35	//axhos_step
36	. *Filter to director service accounts*:
37	.. From `«ACCOUNTS»`, read the `.email` field of each entry and test whether it matches `^${RBCC_account_director}-(.+)@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}$`
38	.. {rbbc_store}
39	`«DIRECTOR_EMAILS»` — the matching emails; per-role observation per BBAAQ contract
40	
41	//axhos_step
42	. *Emit Fact Per Discovered {rbtr_director}*:
43	.. For each `«SA_EMAIL»` in `«DIRECTOR_EMAILS»`:
44	... {rbbc_store}
45	`«IDENTITY»` from the regex capture group — the portion of `«SA_EMAIL»` between `${RBCC_account_director}-` and `@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}`
46	... {rbbc_call}
47	{buf_write_fact_multi}
48	with three positional arguments:
49	.... `<file_root>`: `«IDENTITY»` (bare identity — the basename root for the emitted fact file)
50	.... `<ext>`: `RBCC_fact_ext_roster_director` (resolves at source time to `roster-director`)
51	.... `<value>`: `«SA_EMAIL»` (the full service account email address)
52	
53	//axhos_step
54	. *Report count*:
55	.. {rbbc_show}
56	total count of emitted director fact files
57	
58	//axhoc_completion
59	Successful execution rosters {rbtr_director} service accounts via per-SA fact emission — one `roster-director` fact file per discovered identity, each containing the full SA email as its value.
60	The operation is read-only; no service accounts are created or modified.

content.text:
Now let me examine the finding's core claim closely. The finding asserts that the divest decomposition (`revoke(all held) + remove-citizen`) doesn't pin down roster-deletion ordering, creating an "auto-resurrection" hazard. Let me re-read the relevant memo sections carefully.

The key claims to verify:
1. Line 49: `divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen` — confirmed present.
2. Does the memo specify that remove-citizen deletes the roster entry?
3. Does the audit auto-converge declared-but-absent deficits?
4. Is the ordering hazard real?

Let me check the memo text precisely on these points.

content.content:
40	## Verb dissolution
41	
42	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
43	(actor, capability-set, identity):
44	
45	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
48	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
49	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
50	| roster | read the declared roster | |
51	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
52	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
53	
54	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
55	half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
56	
57	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
58	
59	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster

content.text:
Now I have a thorough understanding. Let me analyze the finding's validity.

**Key analysis:**

The finding's core claim is that the divest decomposition (`revoke(all held) + remove-citizen`, line 49) does not pin down roster-deletion ordering, and that if the roster row survives a revoke, the audit will see declared-but-absent (deficit) and auto-converge, re-granting capabilities to a torn-down identity — "auto-resurrection."

Let me verify each pillar:

1. **Does the audit auto-converge declared-but-absent deficits?** YES — confirmed. Lines 104-105: `deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | ... | auto-converge` and lines 111-112: "Deficit is ours ... → auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant)."

2. **Does the memo state that remove-citizen deletes the roster entry, and pin down ordering?** Let me check. Line 49 just says `revoke(all held) + remove-citizen`. Line 63: "remove = revoke-all then delete the SA." Notably, **neither mentions roster deletion at all.** The memo describes "remove-citizen" purely in terms of SA/key deletion (line 63: "revoke-all then delete the SA"; line 65 for federate: "de-register + revoke-all"). There is no mention of the roster row being deleted as part of remove-citizen for the citizen tier.

3. **Is the hazard real given the audit model?** The audit domain (line 134): "Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings." So the audit iterates over roster identities. If a divested citizen's roster row survives, the audit will: (a) include that identity, (b) compute D = declared sets' bindings (non-empty), (c) compute A = actual bindings (now empty after revoke), (d) classify as deficit-whole, (e) auto-converge — re-grant. But wait — the SA is *deleted*. Can you grant IAM bindings to a deleted SA? That nuances the "resurrection" but the roster→IAM grant would either fail (deleted SA) or, if remove order is revoke-then-the-roster-survives-but-SA-still-being-deleted, there's a window. Actually the more precise hazard: the audit re-grants to a non-existent SA (grant to deleted member fails or creates a phantom binding), OR if a same-name SA is later re-created, it inherits the surviving roster's grants. Either way, a surviving roster row after divest is a real defect.

Let me confirm the memo never mentions roster deletion in the teardown path.

The memo's roster "Writers" section (lines 93-94) covers who *writes* roster entries (grant direction), but the divest/remove-citizen description (lines 49, 63) is silent on roster *deletion*. The grant→intent-first invariant is stated (line 126: "grant writes intent first"), but **the dual — revoke/remove withdraws intent — is not stated.** This is a genuine asymmetry gap.

This substantiates the finding's central claim: the memo specifies "grant writes intent first" but never specifies the dual for teardown, and "remove-citizen" is described only as SA deletion, not roster-row deletion. Combined with the audit auto-converging on deficits over roster identities, a surviving roster row after divest is exactly an auto-resurrection / phantom-grant hazard.

Now let me assess the **severity** and the **fix precision**.

The finding rates this "high" severity (security). Let me weigh: The SA is deleted, so re-granting goes to a now-absent member — the grant either errors or places a binding that has no live holder. The acute "re-provisions a working credential" scenario requires a same-name SA recreation. For citizen tier, identities are operator-supplied folios, so a re-invest of the same identity name (very common: divest then re-invest, which RBSDD's completion note explicitly anticipates — "Proceed to director_invest to re-issue") would land on a surviving roster, and now the audit shows the new SA as already-declared with the *old* union of capability-sets — silently re-granting capabilities the operator may not have re-declared. That is a real privilege-persistence bug. Severity high is defensible but I'd lean medium-to-high; the design is captured (nothing built), and the audit is itself a deferred/future mechanism. Given it's a design-capture review and the hazard is a genuine soundness gap in the asymmetric-heal invariant, I'll rate it **high** — the asymmetric-heal correctness depends on roster being authoritative present intent, and the memo undermines its own invariant by not specifying teardown's roster write.

Let me scrutinize the **fix**. The finding proposes: delete roster row FIRST, then revoke IAM, then remove citizen; add invariant "no IAM revoke without a prior or atomic roster withdrawal."

One refinement concern: the finding's ordering claim ("roster FIRST so no window where declared>actual makes auto-converge re-grant") needs care. If you delete the roster row first, then between roster-delete and revoke there's a window where actual > declared (surplus). But surplus is *reported, not auto-healed* (line 113-116: "Never auto-revoke"). So roster-first creates only a transient surplus, which is safe (no auto-action). Whereas roster-last/never creates a deficit, which auto-converges (dangerous). So roster-first is indeed the correct ordering, and the finding's reasoning holds. The fix is sound.

The fix is well-targeted. I'd sharpen it slightly: the load-bearing point is that **teardown must withdraw intent before (or atomically with) revoking IAM**, making roster-withdrawal the dual of "grant writes intent first." The memo already states the grant-side invariant (line 126) but omits the symmetric teardown-side invariant — that's the precise gap to close.

This is a **real** finding, not yet present in the text, well-substantiated, with a correct fix.

input.reasoning:
I read the memo and the two cited specs. The finding's three load-bearing claims all check out against the source:

1. The divest decomposition is exactly "revoke(all held) + remove-citizen" (memo line 49), and "remove-citizen" is described only in SA/key terms — "remove = revoke-all then delete the SA" (line 63). The memo NEVER mentions deleting the roster row as part of teardown. Confirmed gap.

2. The audit auto-converges declared-but-absent deficits: "deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | ... | auto-converge" (line 104) and "Deficit is ours ... → auto-converge up to D (re-run the idempotent grant)" (lines 111-112). The audit domain is "roster-identities × set-defined bindings" (line 134) — it iterates over roster rows. Confirmed.

3. The memo states the grant-side invariant — "grant writes intent first" (line 126) — but states no dual for teardown. This is a genuine asymmetry in the very invariant the asymmetric-heal rests on (lines 118-122: declared roster = authoritative present intent). A surviving roster row after divest therefore presents as deficit and auto-converges.

So the hazard is real: if a divested identity's roster row survives, the audit re-grants its declared union. Because RBSDD's own completion note anticipates immediate re-invest of the same identity folio ("Proceed to director_invest to re-issue"), the acute case (same-name re-invest landing on a stale roster, silently inheriting the old capability union) is plausible and a privilege-persistence bug, not merely theoretical. The today's-divest spec (RBSDD) is fail-loud and touches no roster (rosters are read-only emission per RBSDR — confirmed: RBSDR is purely "read-only observation ... no service accounts are created or modified"), so the new model genuinely introduces a write-ordering obligation that the decomposition leaves unspecified.

This is NOT already in the text — the memo has no teardown-side intent-withdrawal invariant and no roster-delete step in divest.

The fix is sound: roster-first ordering is correct because the inverse window (roster deleted, IAM not yet revoked) produces only a transient surplus, and surplus is reported-not-auto-healed (lines 113-116), hence safe; whereas roster-last/never produces an auto-converging deficit, which is the dangerous direction. The finding's ordering reasoning is internally consistent with the memo's own asymmetry.

Severity high is defensible: the correctness of the asymmetric-heal design hinges on the roster being authoritative present intent, and the memo undermines its own central invariant by omitting the teardown write. It is design-capture (nothing built), but a soundness gap in the core invariant warrants high.
input.refined_fix: Add the teardown-side dual of the existing "grant writes intent first" invariant (memo line 126), and pin divest ordering. Concretely, in §Verb dissolution / §Identity verbs, specify remove-citizen as: (1) withdraw intent — delete the identity's roster row(s) — FIRST; (2) then revoke IAM (revoke-all); (3) then delete the SA/key. State the invariant explicitly: "no IAM revoke without a prior-or-atomic roster withdrawal — the dual of grant-writes-intent-first." Justify the ordering by the audit's own asymmetry: roster-first leaves only a transient surplus (reported, never auto-healed, lines 113-116), which is safe; roster-last/never leaves a deficit, which auto-converges (lines 104-112) and re-grants a torn-down identity — and because RBSDD anticipates immediate same-folio re-invest, a stale roster would silently re-confer the old capability union on a freshly re-issued SA. Note that line 49's "remove-citizen" and line 63's "delete the SA" currently say nothing about the roster, so the gap is in the decomposition itself, not just the ordering.

content.text: I verified the finding against the memo and the cited specs. It is real: the divest decomposition (memo line 49) and the remove-citizen description (line 63) describe teardown only in SA/key terms and never mention deleting the roster row; meanwhile the audit auto-converges declared-but-absent deficits (lines 104-112) over roster identities (line 134). The memo states the grant-side invariant ("grant writes intent first," line 126) but omits its teardown-side dual, so a surviving roster row after divest presents as a deficit and gets auto-re-granted. The fix (roster-withdrawal first, then revoke, then delete; add the symmetric invariant) is sound, with the refinement that roster-first is provably safe because the inverse window yields only a reported-not-healed surplus. Rated high severity.
